statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Statement-I: In India, prisons are managed by State Governments with their own rules and regulations for the day-to-day administration of prisons
Statement-II: In India, prisons are governed by the Prisons Act, 1894 which expressly kept the subject of prisons in the control of Provincial Governments.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Statement-I: In India, prisons are managed by State Governments with their own rules and regulations for the day-to-day administration of prisons Statement-II: In India, prisons are governed by the Prisons Act, 1894 which expressly kept the subject of prisons in the control of Provincial Governments. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct explanation for Statement- I
options.b: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is not the correct explanation for Statement-I
options.c: Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
options.d: Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **Prisons Act 1894** is one of the oldest pieces of legislation in India dealing with laws enacted in relation to prisons in India.
**Statement 1 is correct:**Prison is a State subject**under List-II of the Seventh Schedule in the Constitution.. States have the primary role, responsibility and power to change the current prison laws, rules and regulations.** Hence it is true that prisons are managed by the state governments with their own rules and regulations.

**Statement 2 is correct: The management and administration of Prisons falls exclusively in the domain of the**State Governments**and is governed by the**Prisons Act, 1894**and the Prison Manuals of the respective State Governments.** Hence it is true that the Prisons Act 1894 kept the subject of prisons in the Provincial government (State governments) and this explains why State governments have the power to frame their own rules and regulations regarding prison management.

statementLines: With reference to the writs issued by the Courts in India, consider the following statements:
1. Mandamus will not lie against a private organisation unless it is entrusted with a public duty.
2. Mandamus will not lie against a Company even though it may be a Government Company.
3. Any public minded person can be a petitioner to move the Court to obtain the writ of Quo Warranto.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the writs issued by the Courts in India, consider the following statements: 1. Mandamus will not lie against a private organisation unless it is entrusted with a public duty. 2. Mandamus will not lie against a Company even though it may be a Government Company. 3. Any public minded person can be a petitioner to move the Court to obtain the writ of Quo Warranto. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct:**Mandamus**is a command issued by the court to a public official asking him to perform his official duties that he has failed or refused to perform.** It can also be issued against any public body, a corporation, an inferior court, a tribunal, or a government for the same purpose.
It is usually not usable against a **private entity**.

**Statement 2 is incorrect:**Mandamus cannot be used against a government company**which is incorporated under the Companies Act as there is neither a statutory nor a public duty imposed on it by a statute in respect of which enforcement could be sought by means of a mandamus. (Wait, the solution text says " Statement 2 is correct" but option (c) says 1 & 3. Adjusting verbatim):**

**Statement 2 is correct: Mandamus cannot be used against a government company which is incorporated under the Companies Act as there is neither a statutory nor a public duty imposed on it by a statute in respect of which enforcement could be sought by means of a mandamus.**

**Statement 3 is correct:**Quo Warranto**is issued by the court to enquire into the legality of the claim of a person to a public office.** Hence, it prevents illegal usurpation of public office by a person.
Unlike the other writs, this can be sought by **any interested person** and not necessarily by the aggrieved person

statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. Pursuant to the report of H.N. Sanyal Committee, the Contempt of Courts Act, 1971 was passed.
2. The Constitution of India empowers the Supreme Court and the High Courts to punish for contempt of themselves.
3. The Constitution of India defines Civil Contempt and Criminal Contempt.
4. In India, the Parliament is vested with the powers to make laws on Contempt of Court.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. Pursuant to the report of H.N. Sanyal Committee, the Contempt of Courts Act, 1971 was passed. 2. The Constitution of India empowers the Supreme Court and the High Courts to punish for contempt of themselves. 3. The Constitution of India defines Civil Contempt and Criminal Contempt. 4. In India, the Parliament is vested with the powers to make laws on Contempt of Court. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct: A committee was set up in**1961**under the chairmanship of the late**H N Sanyal**, the then additional solicitor general.** The committee made a comprehensive examination of the law and problems relating to contempt of court in the light of the position obtained in our own country and various foreign countries.
Pursuant to the recommendations made by the **H N Sanyal Committee**, the **Contempt of Courts Act, 1971** was passed.

**Statement 2 is correct: The**Supreme Court and the High Courts**of India have been empowered with the power to penalize for**Contempt of Court**under**Articles 129 and 215**of the Constitution of India.**

**Statement 3 is incorrect: The Constitution of India**does not define**Civil Contempt and/or Criminal Contempt.**

**Statement 4 is correct:**Article 142 (2)**of the Indian Constitution clearly states that subject to the provisions of any law made on this behalf by Parliament the Supreme Court shall have all and every power to make any order on the punishment of any contempt of itself.** Thus, the **parliament is vested with powers** to make laws on Contempt of Court.

statementLines: With reference to India, consider the following statements:
1. Government law officers and legal firms are recognised as advocates, but corporate lawyers and patent attorneys are excluded from recognition as advocates.
2. Bar Councils have the power to lay down the rules relating to legal education and recognition of law colleges.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to India, consider the following statements: 1. Government law officers and legal firms are recognised as advocates, but corporate lawyers and patent attorneys are excluded from recognition as advocates. 2. Bar Councils have the power to lay down the rules relating to legal education and recognition of law colleges. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect:**Corporate Lawyers**, as well as**patent attorneys**, are too recognized as lawyers and theres**no prohibition**on their recognition as lawyers.**

**Statement 2 is correct: The**Bar Council of India**visits and inspects Universities/Law colleges in the country as part of its statutory function of**promoting legal education**and laying down standards in consultation with the Universities in India and the State Bar Councils.** The Bar Council of India is a **statutory body** established under section 4 of the **Advocates Act 1961** that regulates the legal practice and legal education in India.

statementLines: With reference to India, consider the following statements:
1. Judicial custody means an accused is in the custody of the concerned magistrate and such accused is locked up in police station, not in jail.
2. During judicial custody, the police officer in charge of the case is not allowed to interrogate the suspect without the approval of the court.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to India, consider the following statements: 1. Judicial custody means an accused is in the custody of the concerned magistrate and such accused is locked up in police station, not in jail. 2. During judicial custody, the police officer in charge of the case is not allowed to interrogate the suspect without the approval of the court. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The provisions for custody in India are governed by **Section 167 of the Code of Criminal Procedure**.
A person may be held in the custody of the police or in judicial custody. The first thing that happens to a suspect on arrest is that he is taken into police custody, following which he is taken before a magistrate.
From there, he may either be remanded to judicial custody or be sent back into police custody.

**Statement 1 is incorrect.**Police Custody**means that police has the physical custody of the accused while**Judicial Custody**means an accused is in the custody of the concerned Magistrate.** In Police custody, the accused is lodged in police station lockup while in Judicial Custody, he is locked up in the jail.
**Statement 2 is correct. During Judicial Custody, the**police officer in charge of the case is not allowed to interrogate**the suspect.** However, the **court may allow the interrogations** to be conducted if it opines the interrogation being necessary under the facts produced before the court.
However, during police custody, the police officer in charge of the case, may interrogate the suspect.

statementLines: With reference to India, consider the following statements:
1. When a prisoner makes out a sufficient case, parole cannot be denied to such prisoner because it becomes a matter of his/her right.
2. State Governments have their own Prisoner Release on Parole Rules.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to India, consider the following statements: 1. When a prisoner makes out a sufficient case, parole cannot be denied to such prisoner because it becomes a matter of his/her right. 2. State Governments have their own Prisoner Release on Parole Rules. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect.**Parole**is a system of releasing a prisoner with suspension of the sentence. Parole cannot be claimed as a matter of right**. Parole may be denied to a prisoner even when he makes out sufficient cause for release on parole.
**Statement 2 is correct.**State governments have their own Prisoner Release on Parole Rules**.** Prisons is a **State subject** under the State List of the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution.
The management and administration of prisons fall exclusively in the domain of state governments and are governed by the **Prisons Act, 1894**, and the Prison Manuals of the respective state governments.
The Prisons Act further says that only states can make rules regarding the release of prisoners on furlough, parole and remission, as part of the correctional process in jail reforms.
**

Important Tips
- **Parole** is to be granted only on a sufficient cause such as cases of severe illness or death of any member of the prisoners family.
- The granting authority for parole is the deputy home secretary of the state government.
- On the other hand, **Furlough is seen as a matter of right** for a prisoner, to be granted periodically irrespective of any reason.
- It is granted merely to enable the prisoner to retain family and social ties, and to counter the ill-effects of prolonged time spent in prison.
- Both Parole and Furlough can be denied if the competent authority is of the opinion that releasing the inmate would not be in the interest of society.

statementLines: With reference to Indian Judiciary, consider the following statements:
1. Any retired judge of the Supreme Court of India can be called back to sit and act as a Supreme Court judge by the Chief Justice of India with prior permission of the president of India.
2. A High Court in India has the power to review its own judgement as the Supreme Court does.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Indian Judiciary, consider the following statements: 1. Any retired judge of the Supreme Court of India can be called back to sit and act as a Supreme Court judge by the Chief Justice of India with prior permission of the president of India. 2. A High Court in India has the power to review its own judgement as the Supreme Court does. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct. The**Chief Justice of India**can request a retired judge of the Supreme Court or a retired judge of a high court (duly qualified for appointment as a Supreme Court judge) to act as a judge of the Supreme Court for a temporary period.** He can do so only with the **previous consent of the President** and also of the person to be so appointed.
Such a judge is entitled to such allowances as the president may determine. He will also enjoy all the jurisdiction, powers and privileges of a judge of Supreme Court.
**Statement 2 is incorrect. The Supreme Court of India has power to review its own judgement or order.** Thus, it is not bound by its previous decision and can depart from it in the interest of justice or community welfare.
As a court of record, a High Court also has the power to review and correct its own judgement or order. However, the **power to review of High Court is not similar to the Supreme Court** because the Supreme Courts power to review is consitutionally guaraneted but not in the case of High Court.

statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. The motion to impeach a Judge of the Supreme Court of Indian cannot be rejected by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha as per the Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968.
2. The Constitution of India defines and gives details of what constitutes 'incapacity and proved misbehaviour' of the Judges of the Supreme Court of India.
3. The process of impeachment of the Judges of the Supreme Court of India is given in the Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968.

4. If the motion for the impeachment of a Judge is taken up for voting, the law requires the motion to be backed by each House of the Parliament and supported by a majority of total membership of that House and by not less than two-thirds of total members of that House present and voting.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. The motion to impeach a Judge of the Supreme Court of Indian cannot be rejected by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha as per the Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968. 2. The Constitution of India defines and gives details of what constitutes 'incapacity and proved misbehaviour' of the Judges of the Supreme Court of India. 3. The process of impeachment of the Judges of the Supreme Court of India is given in the Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968. 4. If the motion for the impeachment of a Judge is taken up for voting, the law requires the motion to be backed by each House of the Parliament and supported by a majority of total membership of that House and by not less than two-thirds of total members of that House present and voting. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect. According to provisions of**Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968**, the motion to impeach a judge of the Supreme Court of India**can be rejected by the Speaker**of the Lok Sabha.**

**Statement 2 is incorrect. The**Constitution of India does not define**or give details of what constitutes incapacity and proved misbehaviour of the Judges of the Supreme Court of India.**

**Statement 3 is correct. The**Judges Enquiry Act (**1968**)**regulates the procedure relating to the removal of a judge of the Supreme Court by the process of impeachment.** It states that a removal motion signed by **100 members** (in the case of Lok Sabha) or **50 members** (in the case of Rajya Sabha) is to be given to the Speaker/ Chairman.
**Statement 4 is correct. If the motion for the impeachment of a Judge is taken up for voting, the law requires the motion to be backed by each House of the Parliament and supported by a**majority of total membership**of that House and by**not less than two-thirds of total members**of that House present and voting.** After the motion is passed by each House of Parliament, an address is presented to the president for removal of the judge.
Finally, the president passes an order removing the judge.

statementLines: In India, separation of judiciary from the executive is enjoined by
questions.questionText: In India, separation of judiciary from the executive is enjoined by

options.b: A Directive Principle of State Policy

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP)** is enshrined in Part-IV (Article 36 to 51) of the constitution.
According to **Article 50**, the State shall take steps to **separate the judiciary from the executive** in the public services of the State **

Important Tips-The **Criminal Procedure Code (**1973**)** separated the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the state.
- Prior to this separation, the district authorities like the collector, the sub-divisional officer, the tehsildar and so on used to exercise judicial powers along with the traditional executive powers.
- After the separation, the judicial powers were taken away from these executive authorities and vested in the hands of district judicial magistrates who work under the direct control of the state high court.

statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. The Constitution of India defines its 'Basic Structure' in terms of federalism, secularism, fundamental rights and democracy.
2. The Constitution of India provides for 'Judicial review' to safeguard the citizens' liberties and to preserve the ideals on which the Constitution is based.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. The Constitution of India defines its 'Basic Structure' in terms of federalism, secularism, fundamental rights and democracy. 2. The Constitution of India provides for 'Judicial review' to safeguard the citizens' liberties and to preserve the ideals on which the Constitution is based. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect. The**basic structure doctrine is not defined by the Constitution**.** It is rather a judicial invention that came through **Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala case (**1973**)**.
Supreme Court said that the Constitution of India has certain basic features that cannot be altered or destroyed through the provisions of amendment, by the Parliament of India, as mentioned in the Constitution of India.
**Statement 2 is incorrect. The term**judicial review is not found explicitly**anywhere in the Indian constitution.** However, **Article 13** of the Constitution empowers the SC to review any law in India and strike it down if it violates the Constitutional provisions, especially Part III (on Fundamental Rights), thus providing the power of judicial review indirectly.

statementLines: In India, Legal Services Authorities provide free legal services to which of the following type of citizens?
1. Person with an annual income of less than Rs. 1,00,000.
2. Transgender with an annual income of less than Rs. 2,00,000.
3. Member of Other Backward Classes (OBC) with an annual income of less than Rs. 3,00,000.
4. All Senior Citizens.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: In India, Legal Services Authorities provide free legal services to which of the following type of citizens? 1. Person with an annual income of less than Rs. 1,00,000. 2. Transgender with an annual income of less than Rs. 2,00,000. 3. Member of Other Backward Classes (OBC) with an annual income of less than Rs. 3,00,000. 4. All Senior Citizens. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **National Legal Services Authority (NALSA)** has been constituted under the **Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987** to provide **free Legal Services** to the weaker sections of the society and to organize **Lok Adalats** for amicable settlement of disputes.
The **Income Ceiling Limit** prescribed under Section 12(h) of the Act for availing free legal services depend on the limits set by the respective **State Governments**.
Thus, Senior citizens eligibility for free legal aid depends on the Rules framed by the respective State Governments in this regard. The Income Ceiling Limit prescribed for availing free legal services in is: General
- **Rs. 1,00,000**, Senior citizen
- **Rs. 2,00,000**, Transgender
- **Rs. 2,00,000**.
Free legal aid is available to a member of a **Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe**, not OBC.

statementLines: With reference to the Constitution of India, consider the following statements:
1. No High Court shall have the jurisdiction to declare any central law to be constitutionally invalid.
2. An amendment to the Constitution of India cannot be called into question by the Supreme Court of India.
Which of the statements give above is/ are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Constitution of India, consider the following statements: 1. No High Court shall have the jurisdiction to declare any central law to be constitutionally invalid. 2. An amendment to the Constitution of India cannot be called into question by the Supreme Court of India. Which of the statements give above is/ are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect. With the**power of judicial review**,**High Courts**can strike down State law as well as Central laws.** Though the phrase judicial review has no where been used in the Constitution, the provisions of **Articles 13 and 226** explicitly confer the power of judicial review on a high court.
**Statement 2 is incorrect. The Supreme Court in**Kesavananda Bharati case (**1973**)**held that Constitutional amendment can be challenged on the ground that it violates a fundamental right that forms a part of the**basic structure**of the Constitution and hence, can be declared as void.** Therefore, as it stands the constitutional amendment comes under a law for judicial review if it violates any fundamental rights and **Basic structure** of our constitution.

statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. The 44th Amendment to the Constitution of India introduced an Article placing the election of the Prime Minister beyond judicial review.
2. The Supreme Court of India struck down the 99th Amendment to the Constitution of India as being violative of the independence of judiciary.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. The 44th Amendment to the Constitution of India introduced an Article placing the election of the Prime Minister beyond judicial review. 2. The Supreme Court of India struck down the 99th Amendment to the Constitution of India as being violative of the independence of judiciary. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect. The**44th Amendment**to the Constitution of India had**no provision**placing the election of the Prime Minister beyond judicial review.** 44th Amendment to the Constitution of India was enacted to nullify some of the amendments made by the **42nd Amendment Act, 1976**.
**Statement 2 is correct.**99th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2014**was enacted to reflect the change in the system of appointments of Judges from the collegium system.** It sought to establish the **National Judicial Appointment Commission (NJAC)**. This was struck down by the Supreme Court as being **violative of the independence of judiciary**.

statementLines: In India, Judicial Review implies
questions.questionText: In India, Judicial Review implies

options.a: The power of the Judiciary to pronounce upon the constitutionality of laws and executive orders.
options.b: The power of the Judiciary to question the wisdom of the laws enacted by the Legislatures.
options.c: The power of the Judiciary to review all the legislative enactments before they are assented to by the President.
options.d: The power of the Judiciary to review its own judgements given earlier in similar or different cases.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Judicial Review** is the power of the Courts to determine the **constitutionality of Legislative act and executions orders**.
Judicial review is the power of the judiciary to examine the constitutionality of legislative enactments and executive orders of both the Central and State governments.
On examination, if they are found to be violative of the Constitution (**ultra vires**), they can be declared as **illegal, unconstitutional and invalid** (null and void) by the judiciary.
Consequently, they cannot be enforced by the Government. **

Important Tips The scope of **judicial review** before Indian courts has evolved in three dimensions:
1. to ensure fairness in administrative action. 2. to protect the constitutionally guaranteed fundamental rights of citizens 3. to rule on questions of legislative competence between the centre and the states

statementLines: With reference to the 'Gram Nyayalaya Act', which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. As per the Act, Gram Nyayalayas can hear only civil cases and not criminal cases.
2. The Act allows local social activists as mediators/ reconciliators.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.questionText: With reference to the 'Gram Nyayalaya Act', which of the following statements is/are correct? 1. As per the Act, Gram Nyayalayas can hear only civil cases and not criminal cases. 2. The Act allows local social activists as mediators/ reconciliators. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect.**Gram Nyayalayas**are established under the**Gram Nyayalayas Act, 2008**for speedy and easy access to justice system in the rural areas.** They can try **criminal cases as well as civil suits**, claims or disputes which are specified in the First Schedule and the Second Schedule of the Act.
**Statement 2 is correct. The Act explicitly provides for**mediation by social workers**.** The Gram Nyayalaya shall not be bound by the rules of evidence provided in the **Indian Evidence Act, 1872** but shall be guided by the principles of natural justice and subject to any rule made by the High Court.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Who/Which of the following is the custodian of the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: Who/Which of the following is the custodian of the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The Constitution has conferred a very extensive jurisdiction and vast powers on the **Supreme Court**.
It is not only a Federal Court like the American Supreme Court but also a final court of appeal like the British House of Lords (the Upper House of the British Parliament).
It is also the **final interpreter and guardian/custodian of the Constitution** and guarantor of the fundamental rights of the citizens. The Supreme Court ensures that the other branches of government perform their responsibilities in accordance with the Constitution.
The Supreme Court also ensures that the Constitution of India remains the law of the land and the parliament doesnt affect or in any way undo the soul of the Constitution through its **basic structure doctrine**.
This also ensures that there arent any unreasonable restrictions placed on fundamental rights of individuals by the state.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: What is the provision to safeguard the autonomy of the Supreme Court of India?
1. While appointing the Supreme Court Judges, the President of India has to consult the Chief Justice of India.
2. The Supreme Court Judges can be removed by the Chief Justice of India only.
3. The salaries of the Judges are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India to which the legislature does not have to vote.
4. All appointments of officers and staffs of the Supreme Court of India are made by the Government only after consulting the Chief Justice of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: What is the provision to safeguard the autonomy of the Supreme Court of India? 1. While appointing the Supreme Court Judges, the President of India has to consult the Chief Justice of India. 2. The Supreme Court Judges can be removed by the Chief Justice of India only. 3. The salaries of the Judges are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India to which the legislature does not have to vote. 4. All appointments of officers and staffs of the Supreme Court of India are made by the Government only after consulting the Chief Justice of India. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct. The judges of the Supreme Court are appointed by the**President in consultation with the Chief Justice of India**.** This provision curtails the absolute discretion of the executive as well as ensures that the judicial appointments are not based on any political or practical considerations.
**Statement 2 is incorrect. A judge of the Supreme Court can be**removed from his Office by an order of the president**.** The President can issue the removal order only after an address by Parliament has been presented to him in the same session for such removal.
The address must be supported by a **special majority** of each House of Parliament.

**Statement 3 is correct. The salaries, allowances and pensions of the judges and the staff as well as all the administrative expenses of the Supreme Court are**charged on the Consolidated Fund of India**.** Thus, they are **non-votable** by the Parliament (though they can be discussed by it).
**Statement 4 is incorrect. All appointments of officers and staffs of the Supreme Court of India are**made by the Chief Justice of India**.** To maintain the independence of the judiciary the Chief Justice of India has been given powers to appoint officers and servants of the Supreme Court without any interference from the executive.
He can also prescribe their conditions of service. **

Important Tips The power to increase the number of judges in the Supreme Court of India is vested in the **parliament**.
At present, the Supreme Court consists of thirty-four judges (one chief justice and thirty-three other judges). In **2019**, the centre notified an increase in the number of Supreme Court judges from thirty-one to thirty-four, including the Chief Justice of India.
This followed the enactment of the **Supreme Court (Number of Judges) Amendment Act, 2019**. Originally, the strength of the Supreme Court was fixed at eight (one chief justice and seven other judges).

**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. There are **21 High Courts** in the country, out of these **3 have jurisdiction over more than one state**.

statementLines: Consider the following statements: The Supreme Court of India tenders advice to the President of India on matters of law or fact
1. On its own initiative (on any matter of larger public interest).
2. If he seeks such an advice.
3. Only if the matters relate to the Fundamental Rights of the citizens.
Which of the statement given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: The Supreme Court of India tenders advice to the President of India on matters of law or fact 1. On its own initiative (on any matter of larger public interest). 2. If he seeks such an advice. 3. Only if the matters relate to the Fundamental Rights of the citizens. Which of the statement given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The Supreme Court of India tenders advice to the President of India on matters of law or fact **only if the President seeks such an advice** (and not on its own initiative).
The Constitution (**Article 143**) authorises the president to seek the opinion of the Supreme Court in the two categories of matters: 1) On any question of law or fact of **public importance** which has arisen or which is likely to arise. In this situation, the Supreme Court may tender or may refuse to tender its opinion to the president.
2) On any **dispute arising out of any pre-constitution treaty**, agreement, covenant, engagement, sanad or other similar. In this situation, the Supreme Court must tender its opinion to the president. In both the cases, the opinion expressed by the Supreme Court is **only advisory** and not a judicial pronouncement.
Hence, it is **not binding** on the president; he may follow or may not follow the opinion. However, it facilitates the government to have an authoritative legal opinion on a matter to be decided by it.
The President can seek advice of Supreme Court on matters related to Fundamental rights but it is not the only matter on which President can seek advice.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: With reference to Lok Adalat's, which of the following statements is correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Lok Adalat's, which of the following statements is correct?

options.a: Lok Adalat has the jurisdiction to settle the matters at the pre-litigation stage and not those matters pending before any court.
options.b: Lok Adalat's can deal with matters which are civil and not criminal in nature.
options.c: Every Lok Adalat consists of either serving or retired judicial officers only and not any other person.
options.d: None of the statements given above is correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

Option a is incorrect
- **Lok Adalats** have the jurisdiction to settle both **pre-litigation and pending cases**.
They are an alternative dispute resolution mechanism aimed at resolving disputes outside the regular court process. They can take up cases that are yet to be filed in court (pre-litigation) as well as cases that are already pending before the courts.
Option b is incorrect-While Lok Adalats are more commonly associated with settling civil disputes, they can also handle certain **criminal cases that are compoundable offenses**.
Compoundable offenses are those offenses where the victim and the accused can reach a compromise, and the case can be withdrawn.

Option c is incorrect-While Lok Adalats do have the participation of judicial officers, they are **not exclusively composed of serving or retired judicial officers**.
Lok Adalats typically involve a panel of members, which may include serving or retired judicial officers, lawyers, social workers, or any other person with legal expertise or experience in dispute resolution.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: A Constitution (Amendment) Act may be declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court of India if it-
questions.questionText: A Constitution (Amendment) Act may be declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court of India if it-

options.a: Establishes three-tier federal set up in place of the existing two tier-set up.
options.b: Removes Right of Equality before the law from Part III and places it elsewhere in the Constitution.
options.c: Replaces Parliamentary System of Executive with the Presidential one.
options.d: Establishes a Federal Court of Appeal to lighten the burden of the Supreme Court.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Right to equality** forms part of **basic structure** of Constitution. As per **Keshavananda Bharati case** basic structure of constitution cannot be amended. Hence removing **Right to Equality** from part III and placing it elsewhere in constitution may be declared as unconstitutional by Supreme Court of India.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer was the Chief Justice of India.
2. Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer is considered as one of the progenitors of Public Interest Litigation (PIL) in the Indian Judicial System.
Which of the statement(s) given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer was the Chief Justice of India. 2. Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer is considered as one of the progenitors of Public Interest Litigation (PIL) in the Indian Judicial System. Which of the statement(s) given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Justice V. R. Krishna Iyer** was a judge and Minister who reformed the Indian criminal justice system.
He stood up for the poor and the underprivileged, and remained a human rights champion, a crusader for social justice and the environment, and a doyen of civil liberties, throughout his life.
Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer and **Justice P.N. Bhagwati** are considered the pioneer/ progenitors of **Public Interest Litigation (PIL)**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. The mode of removal of a Judge of a High Court in India is same as that of removal of a Judge of the Supreme Court.
2. After retirement from the office, a permanent Judge of a High Court cannot plead or act in any Court or before any authority in India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. The mode of removal of a Judge of a High Court in India is same as that of removal of a Judge of the Supreme Court. 2. After retirement from the office, a permanent Judge of a High Court cannot plead or act in any Court or before any authority in India. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct-The**mode of removal of a Judge of a High Court**in India is**same**as that of removal of a Judge of the Supreme Court.** Both judges can be removed by a process of impeachment, which is initiated by a motion-a removal motion signed by **100 members** (in the case of Lok Sabha) or **50 members** (in the case of Rajya Sabha) and passed by **Special majority** once admitted by the Presiding officer of the House.
The ground of removal is **proved misbehavior or incapacity**.

**Statement 2 is incorrect-According to the constitution, a**retired High Court judge**is not allowed to practice law in any court or before any authority in India,**except the Supreme Court and other High Courts**.** This restriction implies that the retired judge cannot practice in lower courts or in the High Court from which he/she has retired.
**

Important Tips Some of the provision for independence of Judge of High Court: **Mode of appointment**: The judges of a High Court are appointed by the President of India in consultation with the Chief Justice of India and the Chief Justice of the High Court.
This ensures that the appointment is made on the basis of merit and not on political considerations. **Security of tenure**: The judges of a High Court hold office until they attain the age of **62 years**.
They can only be removed from office by the President on the grounds of proved misbehaviour or incapacity, after an inquiry into the matter by a Supreme Court judge.
This ensures that judges can discharge their duties without fear of being removed from office for political or other reasons **Fixed service conditions**: The salaries, allowances, privileges, leave and pension of the judges of a High Court are determined by Parliament.
These cannot be changed to their disadvantage after their appointment. This ensures that judges can enjoy a reasonable standard of living and do not have to worry about their financial security.
**Expenses charged on Consolidated Fund**: The salaries and allowances of the judges of a High Court are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India.
This means that they are not subject to the annual budgetary process and cannot be reduced or abolished by Parliament. This ensures that the financial independence of the judges is protected.
**Prohibition of discussion in Parliament**: The conduct of the judges of a High Court cannot be discussed in Parliament. This ensures that the judges can discharge their duties without fear of being subjected to political pressure

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. The Parliament cannot enlarge the Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India as its Jurisdiction is limited to that conferred by the Constitution.
2. The officers and servants of the Supreme Court and High Courts are appointed by the concerned Chief Justice, and the administrative expenses are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. The Parliament cannot enlarge the Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India as its Jurisdiction is limited to that conferred by the Constitution. 2. The officers and servants of the Supreme Court and High Courts are appointed by the concerned Chief Justice, and the administrative expenses are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect: Under**Article 138**of the Constitution of India, the**Parliament by law can enlarge**the jurisdiction and power of the Supreme Court on any matter in the Union List.**

**Statement 2 is incorrect: As per**Article 146 and 229**, appointments of officers and servants of the Supreme Court and High Courts are made by their respective**Chief Justices**.** The conditions of service are governed by rules made by the Chief Justices, with salary-related rules requiring the Presidents or Governors approval.
The administrative expenses of the Supreme Court are charged on the **Consolidated Fund of India**, while the administrative expenses of High Courts are charged on the **Consolidated Fund of states**.

options.d: The Union Ministry of Law, Justice and Company Affairs

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Under **Article 138** of the Constitution of India, the **Parliament** by law can enlarge the jurisdiction and power of the Supreme Court on any matter in the Union List.

questions.questionText: The concept of Public Interest Litigation originated in:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The concept of **Public Interest Litigation (PIL)** originated and developed in the **USA in the 1960s**.
In the USA, it was designed to provide legal representation to previously unrepresented groups or interests.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Given below are two statements, one labelled as Assertion (A) and the other labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A): Willful disobedience or non-compliance of Court orders and use of derogatory language about Judicial behaviour amount to Contempt of Court.
Reason (R): Judicial activism cannot be practiced without arming the Judiciary with punitive powers to punish contemptuous behavior.
In the context of the above two statements
which one of the following is correct?
questions.questionText: Given below are two statements, one labelled as Assertion (A) and the other labelled as Reason (R). Assertion (A): Willful disobedience or non-compliance of Court orders and use of derogatory language about Judicial behaviour amount to Contempt of Court. Reason (R): Judicial activism cannot be practiced without arming the Judiciary with punitive powers to punish contemptuous behavior. In the context of the above two statements which one of the following is correct?

options.a: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
options.b: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Assertion (A) is true-Willful disobedience or noncompliance of Court orders and use of derogatory language about Judicial behaviour amount to **Contempt of Court**.
This is because contempt of court is any act that tends to interfere with the administration of justice or to bring the authority and dignity of the court into disrespect.
Reason (R) is true
- **Judicial activism** cannot be practiced without arming the Judiciary with punitive powers to punish contemptuous behavior.
This is because judicial activism is the practice of judges, taking an active role in interpreting the law and issuing rulings that have a significant impact on society.
In order to be able to do this effectively, judges need to have the power to punish people who try to interfere with the administration of justice.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: According to the Constitution of India the term 'district judge' shall not include:
questions.questionText: According to the Constitution of India the term 'district judge' shall not include:

options.d: Chief judge of a small cause court

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. As per **Article 236(a)** of the Constitution, the term **district judge** includes various judicial positions, such as Judge of a city civil court, additional district judge, joint district judge, assistant district judge, chief judge of a small cause court, chief presidency magistrate, additional chief presidency magistrate, session judge, additional session judge, and assistant session judge.
However, it **does not encompass the position of a Tribunal Judge**. Tribunal judges preside over specialized tribunals that deal with specific areas of law and are not part of the regular district judiciary **

Important Tips Articles 233-237 of the Indian Constitution pertain to the regulations governing subordinate courts.
1. **Article 233**
- This article addresses the appointment of district judges.
The Governor of the State, in consultation with the High Court exercising jurisdiction in that State, is responsible for appointing, posting, and promoting district judges.
2. **Article 234**
- Article 234 deals with the recruitment process for individuals who are not district judges but wish to enter the judicial service,
3. **Article 235**
- Article 235 grants the High Court the authority to exercise control over district courts and lower courts.
This includes matters like posting, promotion, and granting leave to individuals in the judicial service of a State who hold positions lower than that of a district judge.
4. **Article 236**
- This article provides the definition of the term judicial service.
5. **Article 237**
- Article 237 empowers the Governor to extend the provisions outlined in this Chapter to specific classes of magistrates within a State.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: When the Chief Justice of a High Court acts in an administrative capacity, he is subject to:
questions.questionText: When the Chief Justice of a High Court acts in an administrative capacity, he is subject to:

options.a: The writ jurisdiction of any of the other judges of the High Court
options.b: Special control exercised by the Chief Justice of India
options.c: Discretionary powers of the Governor of the state
options.d: Special powers provided to the Chief Minister in this regard

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. When the **Chief Justice of a High Court acts in an administrative capacity**, he is subject to the **writ jurisdiction of any of the other judges of the High Court**.
This means that if any action taken by the Chief Justice in his administrative capacity is questioned, any other judge of the High Court can exercise their power of writ jurisdiction to review and potentially correct that action.
**Writ jurisdiction** allows judges to issue writs such as habeas corpus, mandamus, certiorari, prohibition, and quo warranto, which are used to safeguard the fundamental rights of citizens and to ensure that public authorities act within the bounds of law.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which of the following statements regarding the Advisory Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court is correct?
1. It is binding on the Supreme Court to give its opinion on any matter referred to it by the President.
2. The full bench of the Supreme Court hears any reference made to it under its power of Advisory Jurisdiction.
3. The opinion given by the Supreme Court on a reference under Advisory Jurisdiction is not binding on the government.
4. Not more than one reference at a time can be made to the Supreme Court under its power of Advisory Jurisdiction.
Select the answer from the codes given below.
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements regarding the Advisory Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court is correct? 1. It is binding on the Supreme Court to give its opinion on any matter referred to it by the President. 2. The full bench of the Supreme Court hears any reference made to it under its power of Advisory Jurisdiction. 3. The opinion given by the Supreme Court on a reference under Advisory Jurisdiction is not binding on the government. 4. Not more than one reference at a time can be made to the Supreme Court under its power of Advisory Jurisdiction. Select the answer from the codes given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Under **Article 143** of the Constitution the President is authorised to seek the opinion of the Supreme Court on the following matters:
1. Any question of law or fact of **public importance** 2. any dispute arising out of any pre constitution **treaty, agreement, covenant, agreement** etc The supreme court **may give or refuse to give** its opinion in the first case whereas it **must give** its advisory opinion in the second case.
The advice so given is **not binding** on the President. The minimum number of judges to sit on the Constitutional Bench or on Bench which gives its advisory opinion on the reference by the President must be **one half of the total strength** of the Supreme Court.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Given below are two statements, one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other as Reason (R)
Assertion (A): The Supreme Court of India has exclusive jurisdiction regarding Constitutional Validity of Central laws.
Reason (R): The Supreme court is the Guardian of the Constitution of India.
Select the correct answer from the codes given below:
questions.questionText: Given below are two statements, one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other as Reason (R) Assertion (A): The Supreme Court of India has exclusive jurisdiction regarding Constitutional Validity of Central laws. Reason (R): The Supreme court is the Guardian of the Constitution of India. Select the correct answer from the codes given below:

options.a: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
options.b: Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Under **Article 131A** the Supreme Court earlier had exclusive jurisdiction to decide the constitutional validity of a Central law and thus deprived the High Courts of their jurisdiction in respect of the same.
Forty-third Amendment) Act, **1977** omiited Article 131A from the constitution. Hence Assertion(A) is incorrect. The Supreme Court is the highest judicial court in India. It upholds and uplifts the rule of law and ensures and protects citizens rights and liberties as given in the Constitution.
Therefore, the **Supreme Court is also known as the Guardian of the Constitution**. Hence Reason(R) is correct.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Supreme Court judgement regarding homosexuality is related to which of the following?
questions.questionText: Supreme Court judgement regarding homosexuality is related to which of the following?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **Section 377** of the **Indian Penal Code (IPC)** is an act that criminalises homosexuality and was introduced in the ear 1861 during the British rule oflndia.
referred to unnatural offences and says whoever voluntarily has carnal intercourse against the order of nature with any man, woman or animal, shall be punished with imprisonment for life.
**Important Tip** The Supreme Court of India on September 6, **2018**, **decriminalised the Section 377 of the IPC** and allowed gay sex among consenting adults in private.
The SC ruled that consensual adult gay sex is not a crime saying sexual orientation is natural and people have no control over it.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: In India, the power of Judicial Review is enjoyed by?
questions.questionText: In India, the power of Judicial Review is enjoyed by?

options.b: The Supreme Court as well as High Courts

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Judicial review** is the power of the judiciary to examine the constitutionality of executive orders and legislative enactments of both the State and Central governments.
The power of judicial review is itself conferred by the Constitution on the judiciary (**both the Supreme Court as well as High Courts**) in India.
**

Important Tips Judicial review has two important functions, like, of legitimizing government action and the protection of constitution against any undue encroachment by the government.
Judicial review is considered a **basic structure** of the constitution. Judicial review is also called the interpretational and observer roles of the Indian judiciary.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which of the following statements is not true about the power of Supreme Court of India?
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is not true about the power of Supreme Court of India?

options.a: It has the exclusive power to issue writs to protect the fundamental rights of the People.
options.b: It has original and exclusive jurisdiction in inter-governmental disputes.
options.c: It has advisory jurisdiction on a question of law or fact which may be referred to it by the President of India.
options.d: It has power to review its own judgment or order.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Both the Supreme Court and High Courts** can issue writs to protect fundamental rights under the Indian Constitution.
**Supreme Court under Article 32** and **High Courts under Article 226** have this power. **

Important Tips **Articles 226** vests the High Courts with the power to issue writs in case of both-legal and fundamental rights.
Supreme Court only issues writs in cases related to fundamental rights. Hence, the writ jurisdiction of the **High Courts is greater** than that of the Supreme Court.
Supreme Court writ jurisdiction is nationwide while High Courts jurisdiction is limited to its respective state/union territory.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which among the following is not correct about a High Court in India?
questions.questionText: Which among the following is not correct about a High Court in India?

options.a: The second appeal to the High Court is within its appellate jurisdiction.
options.b: Every judge of a High Court is appointed by the President.
options.c: Governor of the State appoints the Judges of a High Court.
options.d: High Courts may accept a petition related to 'Public interest litigation'.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. According to the **Article 217** of the Indian Constitution, a Judge of a High Court is **appointed by the President of India** in consultation with the Chief Justice of India (CJI) and the Governor of the respective state.
The District Court Judges are appointed by the Governor in consultation with the High Courts.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Curative Petition in India can be filed in Supreme Court under Article-
questions.questionText: Curative Petition in India can be filed in Supreme Court under Article-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Article 142** provides a unique power to the Supreme Court, to do complete justice between the parties, where, at times, the law or statute may not provide a remedy.
**Curative Petition** in India can be filed in the Supreme Court under Article 142. It is a special remedy available after the review petitions dismissal, meant to correct errors or injustices during the judicial process, exercised in exceptional cases to prevent abuse of process or miscarriage of justice.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: A Judge of the Supreme Court may resign his office by writing a letter to-
questions.questionText: A Judge of the Supreme Court may resign his office by writing a letter to-
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. A Judge of the Supreme Court may resign his office by writing a letter to the **President of India**, as per **Article 124(2)**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Consider the following statements and state which one of them is correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements and state which one of them is correct?

options.a: Supreme Court of India has only Original Jurisdiction
options.b: It has only Original and Appellate Jurisdiction
options.c: It has only Advisory and Appellate Jurisdiction
options.d: It has Original, Appellate as well as Advisory Jurisdiction

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The Supreme Court of India has three types of jurisdiction: **Original Jurisdiction (Article 131)**: It can directly hear and decide disputes between the Government of India and one or more states, or between different states, involving questions of law or fact on the existence or extent of a legal right, **Appellate Jurisdiction (Articles 132-134)**: It acts as the highest court of appeal, hearing appeals in civil, criminal, and constitutional matters from high courts and tribunals.
**Advisory Jurisdiction (Article 143)**: The President can seek the Supreme Courts advisory opinion on questions of law or public importance, which is not binding.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Under which law is it prescribed that all proceedings in the Supreme Court of India shall be in English language?
questions.questionText: Under which law is it prescribed that all proceedings in the Supreme Court of India shall be in English language?

options.b: Article 145 of the Constitution of India
options.d: Article 348 of the Constitution of India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Article 348 (I)** of the Constitution of India provides that all proceedings in the Supreme Court and every High court shall be in the **English Language** until Parliament by law otherwise provides.
**Important Tip** Part XVII of the Indian Constitution deals with the official languages in Articles 343 to 351, **Article 343 (1)** states that the Official Language of the Union government shall be Hindi in Devanagari script.
Article 351 gives power to the Union Government to issue a directive for the development of the Hindi language. The Hindi language is one of the 22 languages of the Eighth Schedule of the Constitution of India.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The pension of a retired High Court Judge is charged to the-
questions.questionText: The pension of a retired High Court Judge is charged to the-

options.b: Consolidated Fund of the State where he last served.
options.c: Consolidated Fund of the different States where he has served.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The money to pay the **salaries and allowances** of High Court Judges comes from the **Consolidated Fund of the States** where they serve.
On the other hand, when these Judges retire and start receiving **pensions**, the money for their pensions comes from the **Consolidated Fund of India**, which is managed by the central government.
**

Important Tips Expenditures Charged on the **Consolidated Fund of India**: 1. Pensions of the judges of Supreme Court and High Courts. 2. Emoluments and allowances of the Chairman and Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha. 3. Salaries and allowances of the President.
4. Salaries and Allowances of the Supreme Court Judges. 5. Salaries and allowances of the Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: In which of the following cases, the Supreme Court held that, "Fundamental Rights enable a man to chalk out his life in the manner he likes best"?
questions.questionText: In which of the following cases, the Supreme Court held that, "Fundamental Rights enable a man to chalk out his life in the manner he likes best"?

options.b: Golaknath Vs. The State of Punjab

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. In the case of **Golaknath v. State of Punjab (**1967**)**, the Supreme Court of India held that Fundamental Rights enable a man to chalk out his life in the manner he likes best.
This statement signifies the importance of fundamental rights in providing individuals with the freedom and autonomy to live their lives according to their own choices and preferences.
**

Important Tips **Indira Gandhi vs. Raj Narain**: Indira Gandhis election to Lok Sabha was challenged by her opponent, Raj Narain, alleging electoral malpractices.
The Allahabad High Court found her guilty and voided her election. The Supreme Court upheld her conviction. The verdict was nullified by a **1976** amendment during the emergency.
**Bank Nationalization Case**: In R.C. Cooper Vs. Union of India (**1970**), the Supreme Court upheld the nationalization of banks by the government as constitutionally valid.
It emphasized that economic policies are within the scope of parliamentary powers, as long as they dont violate fundamental rights.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which Article of the Constitution permits the Supreme Court to review its judgement or order?
questions.questionText: Which Article of the Constitution permits the Supreme Court to review its judgement or order?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Article 137** of the Indian Constitution permits the Supreme Court to **review its judgments or orders**.
It allows any person aggrieved by a Supreme Court judgment to seek a review of the same. The review petition is heard by a larger bench of the Supreme Court, and it is based on limited grounds such as the discovery of new and important matter or evidence, a mistake or error apparent on the face of the record, or any other sufficient reason.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The Supreme Court of India is a 'Court of Record'. It implies that-
questions.questionText: The Supreme Court of India is a 'Court of Record'. It implies that-

options.a: It has to keep a record of its decisions
options.b: All its decisions have evidentiary value and cannot be questioned in any court
options.c: It has the power to punish for its contempt
options.d: No appeal can be made against its decisions

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. As a **Court of Record** under **Article 129** of the Indian Constitution, the Supreme Courts decisions are **authoritative, have evidentiary value**, and **cant be questioned in other courts**.
Nevertheless, the court can review and correct its judgments through Article 137, acknowledging its potential for errors.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which of the following Courts in India is/ are known as the Court (s) of Record?
questions.questionText: Which of the following Courts in India is/ are known as the Court (s) of Record?

options.c: The High Courts and the Supreme Court

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **Supreme Court and High Courts**, as Courts of Record under **Articles 129 and 215** respectively, record judgments, proceedings, and acts for perpetual memory.
These records hold evidentiary value, cannot be questioned in lower courts, and establish legal precedents. They also have the **power to punish for contempt**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The Supreme Court holds its meetings in New Delhi, but it can meet elsewhere
questions.questionText: The Supreme Court holds its meetings in New Delhi, but it can meet elsewhere

options.b: If most of the Judges of Supreme Court decide.
options.d: On the request of State Legislatur

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. As per **Article 130** of the Indian Constitution, the usual place of the Supreme Courts meetings is in **New Delhi**.
However, the Supreme Court has the flexibility to hold its sessions or sittings at any other place outside New Delhi **if the President of India approves it**.
This provision allows the court to conduct its proceedings in other locations when deemed necessary or in special circumstances with the Presidents permission.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Salaries of the Judges of the Supreme Court are determined by-
questions.questionText: Salaries of the Judges of the Supreme Court are determined by-

options.a: Pay Commission appointed by the President

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Article 125** of the Indian Constitution states that the Salaries of the Judges of the Supreme Court shall be paid as **determined by Parliament** or specified in the Second Schedule until a law is enacted.
Judges shall be entitled to privileges, allowances, leave, and pension as determined by Parliament, or specified in the Second Schedule and these benefits cannot be reduced to their disadvantage after appointment, except in case of financial necessity.
**

Important Tips 1. The Salaries, Pension and Allowances of the Supreme Court Judges are charged upon the **Consolidated Fund of India**, whereas the Salaries and Allowances of the High Court Judges are charged upon the **Consolidated Fund of the States** and the Pension is charged on the Consolidated Fund of India.
2. The High Court Judges (Salaries and Conditions of Service) Act, **1954**, and the Supreme Court Judges (Salaries and Conditions of Service) Act, **1958** regulate the salaries and conditions of service of the judges of High Courts and the Supreme Court of India.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The second largest bench Constituted by the Supreme Court till date was in the-
questions.questionText: The second largest bench Constituted by the Supreme Court till date was in the-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **second largest bench** constituted by the Supreme Court till date was a **11-judge bench** that was constituted iu the case of **Golaknath v. State of Punjab** in **1967**.
**

Important Tips In the **Golaknath Case**, two brothers, Henry and William Golaknath, challenged the Punjab Security and Land Tenures Act that limited their land holdings to thirty acres each, declaring the rest as excess.
The case reached the Supreme Court in **1965** under Article 32 of the Constitution, claiming violation of fundamental rights (Articles 19(f), 19(g), and 14).
In **1967**, the Supreme Court, in a historic judgment, with **6:5 majority** held that **Parliament could not abridge any fundamental rights** guaranteed by the Constitution.
Justice Subba Raos ruling declared the 17th Amendment (which put the Punjab Act in the Ninth Schedule) unconstitutional regarding fundamental rights.
The judgment established the doctrine of basic structure, safeguarding essential features of the Constitution. In response, Parliament passed the **24th Amendment** in **1971**, asserting its power to amend any part of the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights.
The **largest-ever bench** at the Supreme Court of India has been constituted in **1973** in **Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala**. A bench of **13 judges** was set up to decide whether Parliament had the unfettered right to amend the Constitution, which eventually gave rise to the **Basic Structure doctrine**.

However, the only condition being that it must be recorded in the **public interest** rather than entertaining any private interest. It is not necessary, for the exercise of the courts jurisdiction, that the person who is the victim of the violation of his or her right should personally approach the court.

Code:
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements and select the correct answer from the code given: Assertion (A): High Courts are in better position to protect rights of Indian citizens than the Supreme Court. options.b: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not a correct explanation of (A).

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Assertion is true
- **High Courts**, being closer to the ground and having jurisdiction over specific states or union territories, can more effectively address and protect the rights of Indian citizens within their territorial jurisdiction.
They can swiftly respond to local issues and ensure that justice is accessible to the people in their respective regions. Reason (R) is true-The **Supreme Court** can only issue writs to protect fundamental rights under **Article 32** of the Indian Constitution.
However, **High Courts** can issue writs to protect both fundamental rights and other statutory rights under **Article 226** of the Indian Constitution.

statementLines: Acting Chief Justice of India is appointed by the-
questions.questionText: Acting Chief Justice of India is appointed by the-

options.b: Chief Justice of India with previous consent of the President
options.d: President on consultation with the Chief Justice of India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. When the office of the Chief Justice of India (CJI) is vacant or when the CJI is unable to perform their duties due to absence, illness, or any other reason, the **President of India appoints** the most senior judge of the Supreme Court as the **Acting Chief Justice of India**.
However, this appointment is made only after obtaining the previous consent of the outgoing Chief Justice of India. The Acting Chief Justice discharges the functions of the Chief Justice until a new Chief Justice is appointed or the incumbent Chief Justice resumes their duties.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which of the following Articles of the Constitution of India have been declared by the Supreme Court as the 'Inviolable basic structure' of the Constitution:
1. Article 32
2. Article 226
3. Article 227
4. Article 245 Code:
questions.questionText: Which of the following Articles of the Constitution of India have been declared by the Supreme Court as the 'Inviolable basic structure' of the Constitution: 1. Article 32 2. Article 226 3. Article 227 4. Article 245 Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Articles 32, 226, and 227** of the Indian Constitution form the **inviolable basic structure**.
**Article 32** grants the right to approach the Supreme Court directly for the enforcement of fundamental rights. **Article 226** empowers High Courts with the authority to issue writs for fundamental rights enforcement.
**Article 227** confers the power of superintendence over courts and tribunals to High Courts. These articles ensure effective remedies for protecting fundamental rights and maintain the independence and effectiveness of the judiciary, making them crucial components of the Constitutions basic structure.
**Article 245** of the Indian Constitution is primarily concerned with defining the territorial extent of laws made by the Parliament and the State Legislatures.
It deals with the geographical scope within which the laws will apply. While it is an essential provision for the functioning of the legal system, it is **not considered part of the basic structure** of the Constitution.

questions.subject: Polity

statementLines: The Judges of the Supreme Court, after retirement, are permitted to carry on practice before-
questions.questionText: The Judges of the Supreme Court, after retirement, are permitted to carry on practice before-

options.c: Both Supreme Court and High Court

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. After retirement, Judges of the Supreme Court in India are **prohibited from practicing before any court**, including the Supreme Court and High Courts, as per the **Supreme Court Judges (Salaries and Conditions of Service) Act, 1958**. This restriction aims to preserve judicial independence, impartiality, and prevent potential conflicts of interest, ensuring retired judges do not influence legal proceedings and maintain the integrity of the judiciary.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The power of High Court to issue writ covers:
questions.questionText: The power of High Court to issue writ covers:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The power of the High Court to issue writs is a discretionary power that is given to the High Court to protect the fundamental rights of the citizens and to ensure that the government is functioning properly.
The High Court can issue writs to protect **both constitutional rights and statutory rights**. Constitutional rights are rights that are guaranteed by the Constitution of India.
Statutory rights are rights that are granted by a statute or law. The High Court can also issue writs to protect the interests of the public at large.
This is known as the **public interest litigation (PIL)** jurisdiction of the High Court. The PIL jurisdiction allows the High Court to intervene in matters that are of public importance, even if there is no individual who has been directly affected by the matter.
**

Important Tips The writs that can be issued by the High Court are: 1. **Habeas corpus**: This writ is used to ensure that a person who is detained is not being held illegally.
2. **Mandamus**: This writ is used to command a person or authority to perform a public duty. 3. **Prohibition**: This writ is used to prevent a lower court or tribunal from exceeding its jurisdiction.
4. **Quo Warranto**: This writ is used to challenge the right of a person to hold a public office. 5. **Certiorari**: This writ is used to quash the decision of an inferior court or tribunal that has acted without or in excess of its jurisdiction.

statementLines: Who appoints the Judges of the Supreme Court of India?
questions.questionText: Who appoints the Judges of the Supreme Court of India?

options.e: None of the above / more than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Article 124(2)** states that, Every Judge of the Supreme Court shall be **appointed by the President** by warrant under his hand and seal after consultation with such of the Judges of the Supreme Court and of the High Courts in the States as the President may deem necessary for that purpose and shall hold office until he attains the age of sixty-five years

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. A High Court Judge addresses his letter of resignation to the **President of India**. The President is the appointing authority for High Court Judges, and he is also the only authority that can accept a High Court Judges resignation.
