questions.questionText: Supreme Court in India was established-

options.a: By an Act of Parliament in 1950

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The Supreme Court of India was **established by the Constitution of India**, which came into force on 26 January **1950**. The Constitution of India, in **Article 124(1)**, provides for the establishment of a Supreme Court of India.
The Supreme Court is the highest court in India and has original, appellate and advisory jurisdiction.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which one of the following is correct in respect of the appointment of District Judges?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is correct in respect of the appointment of District Judges?

options.a: They are appointed by the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court.
options.b: They are appointed by the President of India on the advice of the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court.
options.c: They are appointed by the Governor in consultation with the High Court of the concerned State.
options.d: They are appointed by the Supreme Court on the advice of the concerned High Court.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The appointment of District Judges is governed by the **District Judges (Conditions of Service) Act, 1958**. This Act states that District Judges shall be **appointed by the Governor of the State** in consultation with the **High Court of the State**.
The Governor is free to consult any other person or authority before making an appointment, but the consultation with the High Court is mandatory.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Who can be appointed as a Judge of the Supreme Court?
questions.questionText: Who can be appointed as a Judge of the Supreme Court?

options.a: An advocate who has practised for at least ten years in any court
options.b: A Judge of a High Court for at least three years
options.c: A distinguished jurist in the opinion of the President of India
options.d: A person who has held a judicial office for at least fifteen years

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Article 124(3)** of the Constitution of India states that a person can be appointed as a Judge of the Supreme Court if he or she is a **distinguished jurist** in the opinion of the President of India.
**Important Tip:** 1. Judges of the Supreme Court are appointed by the President of India according to Article 124(2) of the Constitution. 2. When a judge of the Supreme Court wishes to resign, they submit their resignation to the President. 3. To be eligible for appointment as a judge of the Supreme Court, a person must meet the following qualifications under Article 124(3):
They should be a **citizen of India**. They should have served as a **judge of a High Court for at least five years** (or High courts in succession).
They should have been an **advocate of a High Court for at least ten years** (or High courts in succession). They should be recognized as a **distinguished jurist** in the opinion of the President.
4. The Constitution **does not specify a minimum age requirement** for the appointment of a judge to the Supreme Court.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which one of the following statements about the Supreme Court is not correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements about the Supreme Court is not correct?

options.a: Under Article 129 and Article 144 of the Constitution of India, the Supreme Court has been vested with power to punish for Contempt of court.
options.b: The Supreme Court has been vested with advisory jurisdiction.
options.c: The Supreme Court may refuse to provide opinion to the President, if so asked, in certain circumstances.
options.d: Law declared by the Supreme Court is binding on all courts within the Territory of India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Article 129** establishes the Supreme Court as a court of record with full powers, including the ability to punish for contempt of itself whereas **Article 144** advises civil and judicial authorities to act in aid of the Supreme Court.
**Article 143** of the Indian Constitution is related to the advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which one of the following powers of the Supreme Court is also conferred on a High Court?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following powers of the Supreme Court is also conferred on a High Court?

options.b: Power of doing complete justice in any cause or matter pending before the court
options.d: Power to allow/refuse Special Leave Petitions

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The **power of court of record** is the power conferred on **both the Supreme Court and High Courts**.
As courts of record, they have the authority to keep records of their proceedings, judgments, and orders, The records of the courts are admissible as evidence in other courts.
These records serve as an important source of legal precedent and help maintain the integrity and accountability of the judiciary.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which one of the following authorities CANNOT organize 'Lok Adalats"?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following authorities CANNOT organize 'Lok Adalats"?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Lok Adalats** are informal, speedy, and inexpensive alternative dispute resolution s that are organized by the Legal Services Authorities at the national, state, and district levels.
The **Panchayat Committees are not part of the Legal Services Authorities**. **

Important Tips Lok Adalats have been given statutory status under the **Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987**.
The Lok Adalat have the same powers as are vested in a Civil Court under the **Code of Civil Procedure (**1908**)**. Lok Adalat have the requisite powers to specify its own procedure for the determination of any dispute coming before it.
Under the said Act, the award (decision) made by the Lok Adalats is deemed to be a **decree of a civil court** and is final and binding on all parties and **no appeal** against such an award lies before any court of law.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which one of the following judgements is associated with the primacy of the Chief Justice of India and the Collegium of Judges in the appointment and transfer of the higher judiciary?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following judgements is associated with the primacy of the Chief Justice of India and the Collegium of Judges in the appointment and transfer of the higher judiciary?

options.a: Kesavananda Bharati vs. State of Kerala
options.b: SP Gupta vs. President of India
options.c: Maneka Gandhi vs. Union of India
options.d: SR Bommai vs. Union of India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. In the **SP**Gupta**vs. President of India** case, which is also known as the **First Judges Case**, the Supreme Court of India held that the primacy of the CJTs (Chief Justice of India) recommendation on judicial appointments and transfers can be refused for cogent reasons.
The ruling gave the **Executive primacy over the Judiciary** in judicial appointments for the next 12 years. **

Important Tips 1. **Second Judges Case (**1993**)**:
SC introduced the **Collegium system**, holding that consultation really meant concurrence. It added that it was not the CJIs individual opinion, but an institutional opinion formed in consultation with the two senior-most judges in the SC.
2. **Third Judges Case (**1998**)**: SC on the Presidents reference (**Article 143**) expanded the Collegium to a **five-member body**, comprising the CJI and four of his senior-most colleagues.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which one of the following judgements declared that the Parliament has NO power to amend any of the provisions of Part III of the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following judgements declared that the Parliament has NO power to amend any of the provisions of Part III of the Constitution of India?

options.a: Kesavananda Bharati Vs. State of Kerala
options.b: Golak Nath Vs. State of Punjab
options.c: Champakam Dorairajan Vs. State of Madras
options.d: Minerva Mills Limited Vs.Government of India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The **Golak Nath case** was a landmark judgment of the Supreme Court of India in **1967** that dealt with the issue of the power of Parliament to amend the Constitution.
The Court held that **Parliament could not amend any of the provisions of Part III** of the Constitution, which contains the **Fundamental Rights**.
The Court held that these rights were fundamental and could not be taken away by Parliament. **Important Tip** In the Golak Nath case, the Supreme Court declared the **24th Amendment**, which amended Article 31 regarding the right to property, as unconstitutional.
The Court held that an amendment under Article 368 is law within the meaning of Article 13 of the Constitution and therefore, if an amendment takes away or abridges a Fundamental Right conferred by Part III, it is void.
However, the **Kesavananda Bharati case** later overturned the Golak Nath judgment, stating that while Parliament can amend the Constitution, it cannot destroy its **basic structure**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which one of the following cases in the Supreme Court of India dealt with the issue of 'creamy layer' among the backward classes?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following cases in the Supreme Court of India dealt with the issue of 'creamy layer' among the backward classes?

options.a: KM Nanavati Vs State of Bombay
options.b: Indra Sawhney Vs Union of India
options.d: Sajjan Singh Vs State of Punjab

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The case of **Indra Sawhney Vs Union of India**, commonly known as the **Mandal Commission case**, dealt with the issue of the **creamy layer** among the backward classes in India.
**Important Tip:** The Supreme Court, in its judgment, upheld the validity of reservations for OBCs but introduced the concept of the creamy layer.
It defined the creamy layer as the relatively better-off and more privileged individuals within the OBC category who should be **excluded from the benefits of reservations**.
This exclusion was based on the rationale that reservations should reach those who genuinely need them and uplift the most socio-economically disadvantaged sections.
**KM Nanavati v. State of Bombay** was a case about the right to equality, **Madhu Limaye v. Ved Murti** was a case about the right to freedom of speech, and **Sajjan Singh v. State of Punjab** was a case about the right to property.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The Locus Standi rule to move the court was liberalized by the case of?
questions.questionText: The Locus Standi rule to move the court was liberalized by the case of?

options.a: SR Bommai vs Union of India
options.b: Minerva Mills vs Union of India
options.c: SP Gupta vs Union of India
options.d: Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The locus standi rule to move the court was liberalized by the case of **SP**Gupta**vs Union of India**.
This case is also known as the **Judges Transfer Case**, as it dealt with the issue of transfer of judges from one High Court to another by the President in consultation with the Chief Justice of India.
**Important Tip SR Bommai vs Union of India** was a case that dealt with the constitutional validity of Presidents rule imposed under Article 356 in various states.
The court laid down certain safeguards and limitations on the exercise of this power by the Centre and upheld the federal structure of the Constitution.
**Minerva Mills vs Union of India** was a case that dealt with the constitutional validity of certain amendments made to Article 31C and Article 368 by the 42nd Amendment Act, **1976**. The court struck down these amendments as violative of the basic structure doctrine and upheld the supremacy of judicial review and fundamental rights over directive principles.
**Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala** was a case that dealt with the constitutional validity of certain amendments made to Article 3.1 by various land reform acts.
The court propounded the **basic structure doctrine** and held that Parliament had no power to amend any part of the Constitution that formed its essential core or identity.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which of the following conditions is/are necessary for the issue of a writ of certiorari in India?
1. There should be a tribunal or an officer having legal authority to determine questions affecting rights of subjects and having a duty to act judicially.
2. Such tribunal or officer has acted without jurisdiction.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.questionText: Which of the following conditions is/are necessary for the issue of a writ of certiorari in India? 1. There should be a tribunal or an officer having legal authority to determine questions affecting rights of subjects and having a duty to act judicially. 2. Such tribunal or officer has acted without jurisdiction. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct: The condition mentioned in statement 1 implies that the writ of certiorari can only be issued against a tribunal or an officer who has the legal authority to decide matters that impact the rights of individuals and is obligated to act judicially.** In other words, the tribunal or officer must have the power to make decisions that affect the rights of individuals, and they must have a duty to act in a judicial or quasi-judicial capacity.
**Statement 2 is correct: This condition implies that the writ of certiorari can only be issued if the tribunal or officer has**acted beyond their legal authority or jurisdiction**.** If they have acted without the requisite jurisdiction, the writ can be issued to quash their decision or order.
For the issue of a writ of certiorari in India, both conditions mentioned in options 1 and 2 are necessary. **Important Tip** Concept of **Certiorari** It literally means to be certified or to be informed.
It is issued by a higher court to a lower court or tribunal to either transfer-a case pending with the latter or to overturn the latters order in a case.
It is issued due to an excess of jurisdiction, a lack of jurisdiction, or a legal error. As a result, unlike prohibition, which is only preventative, **certiorari is both preventive and curative**.
Previously, the writ of certiorari could only be issued against judicial and quasi-judicial authorities, not administrative ones. However, the Sipreme Court ruled in **1991** that certiorari c.. be Usued even against administrative authorities affecting individual rights.
Certiorari, like prohibition, is not available against legislative bodies or private individuals or bodies.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Who among the following Chief Justices of India ordered the constitution of a Special Bench called 'Social Justice Bench'?
questions.questionText: Who among the following Chief Justices of India ordered the constitution of a Special Bench called 'Social Justice Bench'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. In December **2014**, when **Justice HL Dattu** was the Chief Justice of India (CJI), the Supreme Court had issued a notification to set up a special Bench known as the **Social Justice Bench**.
The Social Justice Bench was established to exclusively hear cases related to social justice issues. It aimed to expedite the hearing and disposal of cases pertaining to matters such as gender inequality, child rights, disability rights, environmental protection, and other socio-economic concerns.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The issue, 'whether Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code is violative of Articles 21, 14 and 15 of the Constitution of India' was decided in
which one of the following cases?
questions.questionText: The issue, 'whether Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code is violative of Articles 21, 14 and 15 of the Constitution of India' was decided in which one of the following cases?

options.a: Shreya Singhal vs. Union of India
options.c: Shayara Bano vs. Union of India
options.d: Naz Foundation vs. Government of NCT of Delhi and others

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The case of "**Naz Foundation vs. Government of NCT of Delhi and others**" addressed the constitutionality of **Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code**, which criminalized same-sex consensual activities.
The courts decision impacted fundamental rights under **Articles 21, 14, and 15** of the Indian Constitution, including the right to life, equality, and prohibition of discrimination.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Which one of the following writs means 'you may have the body'?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following writs means 'you may have the body'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The writ of **Habeas Corpus** is a legal remedy that allows an individual to seek relief from unlawful detention or imprisonment.
The term Habeas Corpus is derived from Latin, which means **you may have the body. Important Tip** 1. **Article 32** of the Indian Constitution provides for the right to constitutional remedies to protect Fundamental Rights and it is integral to **Basic Structure doctrine**.
2. **Five Constitutional Writs**: **Habeas Corpus**: It used to ensure the release of a person who is unlawfully detained or imprisoned **Mandamus**: It issued to compel public officials or authorities to perform their duties or functions.
**Prohibition**: It is used to prohibit lower courts or tribunals from exceeding their jurisdiction or acting contrary to law. **Certiorari**: It is issued to quash the orders or decisions of lower courts or tribunals on grounds of jurisdictional errors or violation of natural justice.
**Quo Warranto**: It is used to inquire into the legality of a person holding a public office and to challenge their authority or credentials.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Article 231 of the Constitution of India grants power to establish a common High Court for two or more states to
questions.questionText: Article 231 of the Constitution of India grants power to establish a common High Court for two or more states to
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The power to establish a **common High Court** is vested in the **Parliament**, under **Article 231** of the Constitution.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: What is the ground on which the Supreme Court can refuse relief under Article-32?
questions.questionText: What is the ground on which the Supreme Court can refuse relief under Article-32?

options.a: The aggrieved person can get remedy from another court.
options.b: That disputed facts have to be investigated.
options.c: That no Fundamental Right has been infringed.
options.d: That the petitioner has not asked for the proper writ applicable to his/her cas

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Under **Article-32** of the Constitution of India, individuals have the right to move the Supreme Court for the enforcement of their Fundamental Rights.
However, there are certain grounds on which the Supreme Court can refuse relief under Article-32. One such ground is when it is determined that **no Fundamental Right has been infringed** in the case.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: The power of the Supreme Court to decide in the case of a dispute between two or more States is called
questions.questionText: The power of the Supreme Court to decide in the case of a dispute between two or more States is called
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Under **Article 131** of the Constitution of India, the Supreme Court has **original jurisdiction** in matters related to disputes between states, or between the Government of India and one or more states, or between two or more states on one side and the Government of India on the other.
**Important Tip** 1. The Supreme Court has **exclusive original jurisdiction** in federal disputes, which include disputes between the Centre and one or more states, or between different states.
2. This jurisdiction is limited to disputes involving questions of law or fact that determine the existence or extent of a legal right, excluding political questions.
3. The Supreme Court cannot entertain suits brought by private citizens against the Centre or a state under this jurisdiction. 4. The Supreme Courts jurisdiction does not extend to disputes arising from pre-Constitution treaties, agreements, or certain matters such as inter-state water disputes, matters referred to the Finance Commission, commercial disputes, or recovery of damages by a state against the Centre.
5. In **1961**, the Supreme Court dismissed the first suit brought under its original jurisdiction, where West Bengal challenged the Constitutional validity of the Coal Bearing Areas (Acquisition and Development) Act, **1957**, passed by the Parliament.

statementLines: In the Indian judicial system, writs are issued by
questions.questionText: In the Indian judicial system, writs are issued by

options.c: The Supreme Court and High Courts only
options.d: The Supreme Court, High Courts and Lower Courts

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Both the Supreme Court and High Courts** have the authority to issue writs. While the Supreme Court primarily issues writs for the enforcement of fundamental rights, High Courts can issue writs not only for fundamental rights but also for other purposes, such as the enforcement of legal rights or statutory duties.
**Important Tip Article 32**, Constitution of India **1950** 1. The right to move the Supreme Court by appropriate proceedings for the enforcement of the rights conferred by Part III is guaranteed.
2. The Supreme Court shall have power to issue directions or orders or writs, including writs in the nature of habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto and certiorari, whichever may be appropriate, for the enforcement of any of the rights conferred by this Part.
3. Without prejudice to the powers conferred on the Supreme Court by clauses (1) and (2), Parliament may by law empower any other court to exercise within the local limits of its jurisdiction all or any of the powers exercisable by the Supreme Court under clause (2).
The right guaranteed by this article shall not be suspended except as otherwise provided for by this Constitution. Similarily, Under **Art 226**, Constitution of India-High Court have the power to issue writes.
The write jurisdiction of High Court is **wider than that of Supreme Court**, as High Courts can issue writes with respect to Fundamental as well as legal Rights, whereas the supreme court can issue writes with respect to only fundamental rights.

questions.subject: Polity

statementLines: Which one of the following is not the necessary condition for the issue to a Writs of Quo Warranto?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is not the necessary condition for the issue to a Writs of Quo Warranto?

options.a: The Office must be a Public Office.
options.b: The Office must be created by the Statute or by the Constitution itself.
options.c: The Office must not be a substantive one.
options.d: There has been a contravention of the Constitution or a Statute in appropriating such person to that Offic

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. A writ of **Quo Warranto** is a legal action that challenges a persons right to hold a public office.
It seeks to inquire into the authority by which the person claims to hold the office and whether they have the legal right to do so. The reason option (c) is not a necessary condition for issuing a writ of quo warranto is that the eligibility or legality of **holding a substantive office is equally important** to ensure that the person holding the office has the necessary qualifications and meets the requirements laid out by law or the constitution.
**Important Tip Quo Warranto** means **by what warrant**. Through this writ, the Court calls upon a person holding a public office to show under what authority he holds that office.
If it is found that the person is not entitled to hold that office, he may be ousted from it. Its objective is to prevent a person from holding an office he is not entitled to, therefore preventing usurpation of any public office.
It **cannot be issued with respect to a private office**. The writ can be issued only when the following conditions are fulfilled: The public office is wrongfully assumed by the private person. The office was created by the constitution or law and the person holding the office is not qualified to hold the office under the constitution or law.
The term of the public office must be of a permanent nature. The nature of duties arising from the office must be public.

options.a: Bachan Singh Vs State of Punjab (1980)
options.c: Dr. Upendra Baxi Vs State of Uttar Pradesh (1983)

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Option a is the correct. In **Bachan Singh Vs State of Punjab (**1980**)**, Supreme Court laid down that Capital Punishment in India would be given only in the **rarest of rare cases**.
It stated that the principle that the death penalty ought not to be awarded unless the alternative of life imprisonment is unquestionably foreclosed.
**Important Tip Gopalanachari Vs State of Kerala (**1980**)**: Person shall be given legal aid at the expense of the state. **Dr. Upendra Baxi Vs State of Uttar Pradesh (**1983**)**: seeking enforcement of the constitutional rights. **Tukaram Vs State of Maharashtra (**1979**)**: There is a word of difference between sexual intercourse & rape.

statementLines: In
which one of the following cases, the constitutional validity of the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights of Divorce) Act, 1986, was upheld by the Supreme Court of India?
questions.questionText: In which one of the following cases, the constitutional validity of the Muslim Women (Protection of Rights of Divorce) Act, 1986, was upheld by the Supreme Court of India?

options.a: Muhammad Ahmed Khan Vs Shah Bano Begum
options.b: Daniel Latifi Vs Union of India
options.c: Mary Roy vs State of Kerala
options.d: Shankari Prasad Vs Union of India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Daniel Latifi Vs Union of India**: In this case a divorced Muslim women is entitled to reasonable and fair provision and maintenance from her former husband and this should be paid within the period of iddat.
**Muhammad Ahmed Khan Vs Shah Bano Begum**: In this case Supreme Court stated that divorced women had the right to maintenance even after the period of Iddat was over.
**Mary Roy Vs State of Kerala**: landmark case brought equal rights for Syrian Christian women in India as their male siblings on matters of inheritance.
**Shankari Prasad Vs. Union of India**: In this case Constitutional validity of the first amendment act(**1951**) was challenged.

statementLines: The basic structure doctrine with regard to the Constitution of India relates to
1. the power of judicial review.
2. the judgement in Kesavananda Bharati case (1973).
3. the constraints on Article-368 of the Constitution of India.
4. the judgement in Golaknath case (1967).
Which of the statement(s) given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: The basic structure doctrine with regard to the Constitution of India relates to 1. the power of judicial review. 2. the judgement in Kesavananda Bharati case (1973). 3. the constraints on Article-368 of the Constitution of India. 4. the judgement in Golaknath case (1967). Which of the statement(s) given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **basic structure doctrine** was given in the landmark decision of **Kesavananda Bharati v State of Kerala (**1973**)**.
The basic structure doctrine states that the Constitution of India has certain basic features that cannot be altered or destroyed through amendments by The Parliament.
The Supreme Court can undertake **judicial review** for such amendments. The Supreme Court declared that **Article 368** did not enable Parliament to alter the basic structure or framework of the Constitution and Parliament could not use its amending powers under Article 368 alter the basic structure of the Constitution.
Thus,

statement 3 is correct. In the **Golaknath case**, the supreme court ruled that Parliament couldnt curtail any of the Fundamental rights, (this case is not related to basic structure of the constitution).Thus,

statement 4 is incorrect

statementLines: Which one of the following is not the powers of the Supreme Court?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is not the powers of the Supreme Court?

options.a: The Supreme Court has original and exclusive jurisdiction in inter-governmental disputes.
options.b: The Supreme Court has advisory jurisdiction on a question of law or fact which may be referred to it by the President of India
options.c: The Supreme Court has the power to review its own judgement or order
options.d: The Supreme Court has the exclusive power to issue writs to protect the fundamental rights of the peopl

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The Supreme Court is the guarantor and defender of fundamental rights of the citizen. In this regard, Supreme Court has original jurisdiction in the sense that an aggrieved citizen can directly go to the Supreme Court, not necessarily by way of appeal.
However, the writ jurisdiction of Supreme Court is **not exclusive** because **High Court also empowered of issue writ jurisdiction** (Hence

option d is incorrect)

statementLines: In which of the following cases did the Supreme Court rule that Constitutional Amendments were also laws under Article-13 of the Constitution of India, which could be declared void for being inconsistent with Fundamental Rights?
questions.questionText: In which of the following cases did the Supreme Court rule that Constitutional Amendments were also laws under Article-13 of the Constitution of India, which could be declared void for being inconsistent with Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Golak Nath case** held that constitutional amendment passed according to **article 368 is a law** within the meaning of article 13.
**24th amendment act**1971**override this judgement** and declare that amendment under article 368 is not law within the meaning of article 13.
**Important Tip Keshavanand Bharati Case** is related to the basic structure doctrine. **Minerva Mills Case** is related to the interpretation of the basic structure doctrine.
**Maneka Gandhi Case** is related to the Right of Personal Liberty granted by Article 21 of the Constitution.

statementLines: In the following questions, there are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and other is labelled as Reason (R):
Assertion (A): The District Judge is the highest judicial authority in the district.
Reason (R): The District Judge has both judicial and administrative powers. He has also supervisory powers over all the subordinate courts in the district.
In the context of the above two statements which of the following is correct?
questions.questionText: In the following questions, there are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and other is labelled as Reason (R): Assertion (A): The District Judge is the highest judicial authority in the district. Reason (R): The District Judge has both judicial and administrative powers. He has also supervisory powers over all the subordinate courts in the district. In the context of the above two statements which of the following is correct?

options.a: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, and Reason (R) is correct explanation of Assertion (A)
options.b: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not correct explanation of Assertion (A)
options.c: Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false
options.d: Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Assertion is true-In the district, the **district judge holds the highest judicial authority**, having both original and appellate jurisdiction over both civil and criminal matters.
Part VI (Articles 233 to 237) of the Indian Constitution deals with the District Courts. Reason (R) is true-The district judge exercises **both judicial and administrative powers**, and they also possess **supervisory authority** over all subordinate courts within the district.
The High Court hears appeals against the directives and judgments of the district judge.

statementLines: Judicial review in the Indian Constitution is based on-
questions.questionText: Judicial review in the Indian Constitution is based on-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Judicial Review is governed by the principle of **Procedure established by law** Judicial review is the power of the judiciary to examine the constitutionality of executive orders and legislative enactments of both the State and Central governments.
If, after this examination, they are found to be violative of the Constitution, they can be declared as unconstitutional, illegal, and invalid (null and void) by the judiciary.

statementLines: A Judge of the Supreme Court can be removed by the President of India after-
questions.questionText: A Judge of the Supreme Court can be removed by the President of India after-

options.b: An enquiry by Chief Justice of India
options.c: A report by the Bar Council of India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. A Judge of the Supreme Court can be removed by the President of India after an **impeachment by the Parliament**.
As per **Article 124(4)**, A Judge of the Supreme Court can be removed from office by the President, of India only after an address by each House of Parliament supported by a majority of the total membership of that House and by a **majority of not less than two-thirds** of members present and voting, and presented to the President in the same session for such removal on the ground of **proved misbehaviour or incapacity**.
**

Important Tips Article 124(4) and the Judges Inquiry Act **1968** determine the procedure of removal of the judges: A motion of impeachment addressed to the President is to be signed by at least **100 members of the Lok Sabha or 50 members of the Rajya Sabha** and then delivered to the Speaker of Lok Sabha or the Chairman of Rajya Sabha.
The motion is to be investigated by a **Committee of 3 judges** of the Supreme Court and a distinguished jurist. If the Committee finds the judge guilty of misbehavior or that he suffers from incapacity, the motion along with the report of the committee is taken up for consideration in the House where motion was moved.
The judge is then removed by the requisite majority, i.e. majority of total and 2/3 of its members present and voting.

statementLines: Which of the following type of Judge/Judges can be appointed in the Supreme Court of India?
1. (i) Ad hoc Judge
2. (ii) Additional Judge Codes:
questions.questionText: Which of the following type of Judge/Judges can be appointed in the Supreme Court of India? 1. (i) Ad hoc Judge 2. (ii) Additional Judge Codes:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Option (i) is True: As per **Article 127(1)**, if theres no quorum of Judges available for a Supreme Court session, the Chief Justice of India, with the Presidents prior consent and consultation with the relevant High Courts Chief Justice, may request a qualified High Court Judge to serve as an **ad hoc Judge** for the required period.
Option (ii) is False: As per **Article 224(1)**, if theres a temporary increase in a High Courts business or arrears of work, the President may appoint qualified persons as **additional Judges** for up to two years.
However, **additional judges are not appointed in the Supreme Court; they are appointed in High Courts**.

statementLines: Who has the right to transfer any case anywhere in India?
questions.questionText: Who has the right to transfer any case anywhere in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. According to **Article 139A** of the Constitution of India the **Supreme Court** has the right to transfer any case anywhere in the territory of India.
The Supreme Court can also withdraw the case or cases pending before the High Court and dispose of all the cases to itself.

statementLines: To become a Judge of Supreme Court, a person must be an advocate in High Court for at least how many years?
questions.questionText: To become a Judge of Supreme Court, a person must be an advocate in High Court for at least how many years?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. According to **Article 124(3)** of the Constitution of India, a person can be appointed as a Judge of the Supreme Court if he/she has been an **advocate of a High Court** or of two or more such Courts in succession for at least **10 years**.
**

Important Tips According to Article 124(3) of the Constitution, a person can be appointed as a judge of the Supreme Court if he or she: A person must be a **citizen of India**. Must have served as a **judge of a High Court for at least five years** or two such courts in succession.
Alternatively, must have been an **advocate of a High Court for at least ten years** or two or more such courts in succession. Must be a **distinguished jurist** in the opinion of the president.

statementLines: The case of Vishakha and others Vs. State of Rajasthan and others is related to
questions.questionText: The case of Vishakha and others Vs. State of Rajasthan and others is related to

options.a: Maternity leave in respect of working women
options.c: Prevention of the practice of dowry prevalent in the society
options.d: Prevention of sexual harassment of women at workplace

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Vishaka & ors. v/s state of Rajasthan** is a case which deals with the evil of **Sexual Harassment of a women at her workplace**.
It is a landmark judgment case in the history of sexual harassment which as being decide by Supreme Court. **

Important Tips In **1997**, the Supreme Court in India formulated the **Vishakha guidelines** to protect women from sexual harassment at workplaces.
The Supreme court made it mandatory for all institutions to follow the Vishaka guidelines across the country. The guidelines suggested that the compliance mechanism should ensure the timebound treatment of complaints.
The Vishakha guidelines gave rise to the foundation to redress the **Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition, and Redressal) Act, 2013**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Identify the incorrect pair of Case and Ruling in that case by Supreme Court:
questions.questionText: Identify the incorrect pair of Case and Ruling in that case by Supreme Court:

options.a: Indira Sawhney Case: Creamy Layer for Other Backward Classes.
options.b: Vishakha Case: Protection of working women against sexual harassment at their place of work.
options.c: Maneka Gandhi Case: Articles 14, 19 and 21 are not mutually exclusive.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Bella Banerjee case (**1953**)** dealt with the constitutionality of the **West Bengal Land Development and Planning Act, 1948**. The Supreme Court held certain provisions of the Act unconstitutional and void.
The judgment highlighted the importance of providing fair compensation to landowners during the process of land acquisition for public purposes and established the principle that the governments declaration of public purpose should not be regarded as conclusive, leaving room for judicial review.
It **did not concern the right to travel abroad** as part of personal liberty. **

Important Tips The **Indira Sawhney case**, also known as the Mandal Commission case, **1992** where the Supreme Court upheld the implementation of reservations for Other Backward Classes (OBCs) in educational institutions and public employment.
However, the Court ruled that the **creamy layer** within OBCs should be excluded from reservation benefits to ensure that the benefits reach the truly disadvantaged sections.
In the **Vishakha case** of **1997**, the Supreme Court laid down guidelines to prevent sexual harassment of women at workplaces. These guidelines were later incorporated into the Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition, and Redressal) Act, **2013**.
In the **Maneka Gandhi case (**1978**)**, the Supreme Court held that the right to life and personal liberty (Article 21) in the Indian Constitution is not limited to mere animal existence but includes a meaningful life with dignity.
The Court emphasized that **Articles 14, 19, and 21 are not mutually exclusive** and are interconnected to ensure fundamental rights comprehensive protection.

statementLines: All the cases regarding interpretation of the Constitution can be brought to the Supreme Court under its-
questions.questionText: All the cases regarding interpretation of the Constitution can be brought to the Supreme Court under its-
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The **appellate jurisdiction** of the Supreme Court can be invoked by a certificate granted by the High Court concerned under **Article 132(1), 133(1) or 134** of the Constitution in respect of any judgement, decree or final order of a High Court in both civil and criminal cases, involving **substantial questions of law as to the interpretation of the Constitution**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Where can the Public Interest Litigation (PIL) be filed?
questions.questionText: Where can the Public Interest Litigation (PIL) be filed?

options.a: Only in Supreme Court of India
options.b: Only in High Courts of States
options.d: Both in High Courts & Supreme Court

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. A **Public Interest Litigation** can be filed in the **High Court or Supreme Court**. One can file a PIL under **Article 226** of the Constitution before the High Court and under **Article 32** before the Supreme Court of India.
**

Important Tips Public interest Litigation (PIL) means litigation filed in a court of law, for the protection of "**Public Interest**, such as Pollution, Terrorism, Road safety, Constructional hazards etc.
Any matter where the interest of public at large is affected can be redressed by filing a Public Interest Litigation in a court of law. Public interest litigation is **not defined** in any statute or in any act. It has been interpreted by judges to consider the intent of public at large.
